abstract: We introduce Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 (FDB-v3), a benchmark for evaluating spoken language models under naturalistic speech conditions and multi-step tool use. Unlike prior work, our dataset consists entirely of real human audio annotated for five disfluency categories, paired with scenarios requiring chained API calls across four task domains. We evaluate six model configurations -- GPT-Realtime, Gemini Live 2.5, Gemini Live 3.1, Grok, Ultravox v0.7, and a traditional Cascaded pipeline (Whisper$\rightarrow$GPT-4o$\rightarrow$TTS) -- across accuracy, latency, and turn-taking dimensions. GPT-Realtime leads on Pass@1 (0.600) and interruption avoidance (13.5\%); Gemini Live 3.1 achieves the fastest latency (4.25~s) but the lowest turn-take rate (78.0\%); and the Cascaded baseline, despite a perfect turn-take rate, incurs the highest latency (10.12~s). Across all systems, self-correction handling and multi-step reasoning under hard scenarios remain the most consistent failure modes.

citations.fullCitation: Guan-Ting Lin, Shih-Yun Shan Kuan, Jiatong Shi, Kai-Wei Chang, Siddhant Arora, Shinji Watanabe, and Hung-yi Lee. 2025a. Full-duplex-bench-v2: A multi-turn evaluation framework for duplex dialogue systems with an automated examiner. arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.07838.
citations.justification: This paper is the direct predecessor to the work being presented. FDB-v3 is an explicit extension of the Full-Duplex-Bench series, and this citation provides the necessary context for the new version's contributions, which are adding real human audio and multi-step tool use.
citations.title: Full-duplex-bench-v2: A multi-turn evaluation framework for duplex dialogue systems with an automated examiner

citations.fullCitation: Dhruv Jain, Harshit Shukla, Gautam Rajeev, Ashish Kulkarni, Chandra Khatri, and Shubham Agarwal. 2025. Voiceagentbench: Are voice assistants ready for agentic tasks? arXiv preprint arXiv:2510.07978.
citations.justification: This citation is used as a key point of contrast to highlight a gap in existing research. The authors mention that VoiceAgentBench evaluates complex tool-use but relies on synthetic audio, which justifies their paper's focus on using authentic, disfluent human speech.
citations.title: Voiceagentbench: Are voice assistants ready for agentic tasks?

citations.fullCitation: Advait Gosai, Tyler Vuong, Utkarsh Tyagi, Steven Li, Wenjia You, Miheer Bavare, Arda Uçar, Zhongwang Fang, Brian Jang, Bing Liu, and 1 others. 2025. Audio multichallenge: A multi-turn evaluation of spoken dialogue systems on natural human interaction. arXiv preprint arXiv:2512.14865.
citations.justification: This paper is cited to demonstrate the limitations of benchmarks that use real speech but lack tool-use evaluation. By referencing this work, the authors establish the novelty of FDB-v3, which uniquely combines authentic human speech with complex, multi-step tool-use scenarios.
citations.title: Audio multichallenge: A multi-turn evaluation of spoken dialogue systems on natural human interaction

citations.fullCitation: Mattias Heldner and Jens Edlund. 2010. Pauses, gaps and overlaps in conversations. Journal of Phonetics, 38(4):555–568.
citations.justification: This foundational paper is cited in the introduction to provide the scientific rationale for why latency is a critical evaluation metric. It establishes that a spoken reply must arrive within a narrow time window to feel natural, grounding the paper's focus on latency and turn-taking in established research.
citations.title: Pauses, gaps and overlaps in conversations

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Analysis: Full-Duplex-Bench-v3: Benchmarking Tool Use for Full-Duplex Voice Agents Under Real-World Disfluency

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper was authored by Guan-Ting Lin and Hung-yi Lee from National Taiwan University, and Chen Chen and Zhehuai Chen from NVIDIA.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) with external Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) has enabled text-based AI systems to perform complex, multi-step tasks, transitioning them into autonomous assistants. However, extending these tool-use capabilities to voice agents has progressed at a slower rate. Most existing spoken dialogue systems primarily engage in conversational chat without the capacity to invoke external APIs or execute actions on behalf of the user. This limitation is particularly pertinent for voice agents, which are often deployed in scenarios requiring practical actions, such as checking prices or updating settings.

A significant challenge in voice interaction is latency. Unlike text-based interactions, spoken replies must be delivered within a narrow timeframe to maintain a natural conversational flow, creating a tension between thorough reasoning and prompt response. Prior research into combining tool use with low conversational latency has faced several limitations. Early cascaded approaches, such as AudioGPT and Speech-Copilot, facilitated LLM-orchestrated tool use for speech tasks but were designed for offline processing, precluding real-time dialogue. More recent efforts like StreamRAG and SHANKS aim for conversational settings but often rely on synthetic data for training and are not publicly accessible, hindering independent verification of their real-world performance. Proprietary systems like OpenAI’s GPT-Realtime API and Google’s Gemini Live are considered capable but lack evaluation under controlled, reproducible conditions.

Existing benchmarks also present inadequacies for evaluating real-world tool execution in voice agents. While the Full-Duplex-Bench series (v1, v1.5, v2) initiated evaluations for turn-taking, overlaps, and multi-turn dialogues, these often utilize Text-to-Speech (TTS) synthesized audio rather than authentic human queries. Benchmarks featuring real speech, such as Audio MultiChallenge and WildSpeech-Bench, typically omit tool-use evaluations. Conversely, benchmarks that do assess tool use—including τ-Voice, AudioCRAG, and VoiceAgentBench—either employ synthetic audio, restrict tasks to single-step actions, or do not account for the natural disfluencies inherent in human spoken interaction. This oversight means that the impact of natural speech phenomena, such as mid-utterance self-corrections or false starts, on programmatic state updates and tool execution has largely remained unaddressed in structured evaluations.

This research introduces Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 (FDB-v3) to bridge these identified gaps. It aims to provide a benchmark for evaluating voice agents on multi-step tool execution under naturalistic speech conditions, incorporating real human audio with systematic disfluency annotations, and scenarios that test dynamic state updates and chained API calls. This work seeks to provide a controlled environment for assessing both proprietary and open-weight models against a comprehensive set of real-world interaction challenges, thereby advancing the understanding and development of robust, real-time, tool-using voice agents.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to introduce Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 (FDB-v3), a novel benchmark designed for evaluating spoken language models under naturalistic speech conditions and multi-step tool use scenarios. This benchmark aims to provide a comprehensive and reproducible framework for assessing the performance of full-duplex voice agents.

The motivation for developing FDB-v3 stems from several critical gaps identified in the existing research landscape:

1.  **Lack of Authentic Human Speech**: Prior benchmarks often rely on Text-to-Speech (TTS) synthesized audio, which fails to capture the nuances and variability of real human speech. Natural speech is characterized by disfluencies such as fillers, pauses, hesitations, false starts, and self-corrections. Evaluating models with artificially generated speech does not accurately reflect their robustness in real-world interactions. Therefore, a key motivation was to create a dataset composed entirely of real human audio, systematically annotated for these disfluency categories.

2.  **Inadequate Handling of Dynamic State Updates**: Human conversation is often iterative and subject to change. Users may modify their intent mid-utterance, requiring the voice agent to dynamically update its internal state and adjust subsequent API parameters. Existing tool-use benchmarks frequently simplify this aspect by focusing on single-step actions or lacking scenarios that test programmatic state rollback. A specific objective was to include scenarios that specifically test an agent's ability to recognize and correctly process self-corrections, discarding obsolete context and updating downstream API parameters.

3.  **Limited Scope of Tool-Use Tasks**: Many existing tool-use evaluations are restricted to single-step actions, which does not reflect the complexity of real-world agentic tasks. To address this, a motivation was to design scenarios requiring chained API calls across diverse task domains (e.g., Travel & Identity, Finance & Billing, Housing & Location, E-Commerce Support). This multi-step functionality is crucial for agents to orchestrate complex workflows.

4.  **Absence of Controlled Evaluation for Proprietary Systems**: The most capable real-time voice agents are often proprietary and their performance has not been rigorously evaluated under controlled, reproducible conditions. This lack of transparency hinders a fair comparison and understanding of their strengths and weaknesses. FDB-v3 aims to provide an open benchmark to assess these systems alongside open-weight alternatives.

5.  **Comprehensive Assessment Beyond Accuracy**: Evaluating voice agents solely on tool-use accuracy is insufficient. Real-time interaction introduces critical dimensions such as latency and turn-taking dynamics. The objective was to provide a multi-dimensional evaluation, encompassing accuracy (tool selection, argument accuracy, task completion), latency (first response, tool call, task completion), and conversational flow (turn-take rate, interruption rate, filler usage).

In essence, FDB-v3 is motivated by the need for a realistic, challenging, and comprehensive evaluation framework that pushes the boundaries of current voice agent capabilities by exposing them to the complexities of natural human speech and multi-step, dynamic tool execution in real-time.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The development of Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 (FDB-v3) involved the creation of a specialized dataset and a multi-dimensional evaluation framework.

**4.1. Task Domains and Mock APIs**
The benchmark utilizes locally executed mock APIs with deterministic, zero-latency responses. This approach isolates the model's reasoning and parameter-passing abilities from external confounds like network variability or service downtime, ensuring that measured latencies reflect solely the model’s processing overhead. The mock APIs are designed to provide fully deterministic outputs, facilitating automatic scoring. The benchmark spans four task domains: Travel & Identity, Finance & Billing, Housing & Location, and E-Commerce Support, each equipped with a small set of callable tools.

**4.2. Dataset Preparation and Difficulty Tiers**
Scenarios within the dataset are categorized into three difficulty tiers:
*   **Easy**: Involves single-step tool calls.
*   **Medium**: Requires two-step tool calls with moderate ambiguity.
*   **Hard**: Demands multi-step tool calls with potentially conflicting constraints, challenging complex reasoning.
A total of 100 scenarios were formulated, with 21 specifically designed to feature self-correction events to test real-time state rollback capabilities.

**4.3. Audio Data Collection and Disfluency Annotation**
All audio data was collected from human speakers in uncontrolled environments. The dataset comprises 100 recordings from 12 speakers, including both native and non-native English speakers with varying accent strengths (Korean and Russian backgrounds). Speakers were provided with detailed scenario contexts and instructed to perform prompts organically. Audio capture utilized everyday built-in microphones (11 out of 12 setups) in environments ranging from quiet rooms to those with mild background noise, aiming for realistic acoustic conditions.

Each recording was systematically annotated for five disfluency categories to target specific failure modes:
*   **False Starts**: Assess the model's ability to discard obsolete context without hallucinating tool calls when an intent is abandoned for a new one.
*   **Self-Corrections**: Evaluate dynamic state rollback, specifically updating parameters mid-sentence.
*   **Fillers** (e.g., "um," "uh"): Probe whether redundant tokens degrade accuracy or inflate latency.
*   **Pauses**: Test the robustness of end-of-turn detection during mid-utterance silences.
*   **Hesitations**: Evaluate handling of filler-repetition combinations.
For trailing silence, 30 seconds of the actual ambient environment of each speaker were captured rather than appending digital silence, maintaining acoustic coherence. Each speaker was assigned 10 scenarios distributed across all four domains, with disfluencies proportionally represented.

**4.4. Experimental Setup**
Six model configurations were evaluated: GPT-Realtime, Gemini Live 2.5, Gemini Live 3.1, Grok, Ultravox v0.7, and a traditional Cascaded baseline. All systems were deployed through the LiveKit Realtime Voice Agent framework for streaming audio and real-time tool use. The first five are end-to-end speech-to-speech models, while the Cascaded system employs a modular pipeline: OpenAI Whisper for speech recognition, GPT-4o for reasoning and tool use, and OpenAI TTS for speech synthesis. Each model received identical audio input.

**4.5. Evaluation Metrics**
Agents were scored across four dimensions:
*   **Tool-Use Performance**:
    *   **Tool Selection F1**: F1 score based on expected vs. actual tool calls.
    *   **Argument Accuracy**: Semantic correctness of generated arguments, judged by GPT-4o to accommodate valid input variations.
    *   **Task Completion (Pass@1)**: Binary metric requiring exact expected tool invocations and perfect argument accuracy for all calls.
*   **Response Quality**: GPT-4o judged whether the agent’s spoken transcript accurately fulfilled the user’s intent in natural language.
*   **Turn-Taking and Latency Dynamics**:
    *   **Turn-take rate**: Fraction of turns with a natural-timing response.
    *   **Base Latency**: Time from user utterance end to agent response start (∆t = t_agent_start - t_user_end).
    *   **Interruption**: Occurs if ∆t < 0.
    *   **Filler Rate**: Percentage of responses containing content-free filler sentences.
*   **Latency Decomposition**:
    *   **First Response Latency**: Time until any speech, including fillers.
    *   **Tool Call Latency**: Time until the first API invocation.
    *   **Task Completion Latency**: Time until the agent delivers the factual answer, isolating informational content from filler speech using GPT-4o analysis.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The evaluation of six distinct model configurations across FDB-v3 revealed varied performance characteristics concerning accuracy, latency, and conversational dynamics.

**5.1. Overall Performance**
GPT-Realtime demonstrated the strongest overall performance, leading across several key metrics: tool selection F1 (0.876), argument accuracy (0.680), response quality (0.792), and Task Completion (Pass@1) at 0.600. It also exhibited the lowest interruption rate (13.5%). Gemini Live 3.1 achieved the fastest task completion latency (4.25 s) but had the lowest turn-take rate (78.0%), indicating it failed to respond in 22% of scenarios. Gemini Live 2.5 was more conservative with a higher turn-take rate (92.0%) and low interruption (14.1%) but lower overall accuracy. Grok and Ultravox showed lower accuracy tiers (Pass@1 0.430 and 0.410, respectively). Ultravox exhibited a high interruption rate (47.9%) and filler rate (88.0%), often speaking over users with content-free utterances. The Cascaded baseline, despite a perfect turn-take rate (100%), incurred the highest latency (10.12 s) and a 33.0% interruption rate.

**5.2. Performance by Difficulty**
Performance consistently degraded as scenario complexity increased across all evaluated systems. GPT-Realtime maintained its lead across all difficulty tiers, scoring 0.750 on Easy, 0.588 on Medium, and 0.433 on Hard tasks. The Cascaded baseline performed competitively on Easy tasks (0.639) but showed a sharp decline on Hard scenarios (0.233), suggesting that accumulated ASR errors become more impactful with task complexity. Grok exhibited the steepest decline in performance on Hard scenarios (0.200), underscoring the challenge of multi-step reasoning under disfluent speech conditions.

**5.3. Robustness to Disfluency**
GPT-Realtime either led or tied in performance across all disfluency categories. It achieved a notable self-correction score of 0.588, surpassing all other systems. Gemini Live 2.5 followed at 0.471 for self-corrections. Gemini Live 3.1, while generally improved, trailed at 0.353 for self-corrections, indicating that its architectural updates may not have specifically addressed state rollback. Handling pauses proved to be a common challenge, with Grok and Ultravox both scoring 0.333 in this category.

**5.4. Performance by Domain**
All models found the Finance domain to be the easiest, with GPT-Realtime achieving 0.960 and Gemini Live 3.1 scoring 0.920. Conversely, the Housing domain was consistently the most challenging, where GPT-Realtime led at 0.308 and Grok scored 0.115. GPT-Realtime maintained its leading position across all four task domains. Ultravox exhibited domain imbalance, with particularly low scores in E-commerce (0.345) and Housing (0.192), indicating potential weaknesses in handling multi-entity order processes and complex constraint reasoning.

**5.5. Latency Analysis**
Gemini Live 3.1 recorded the fastest latencies across all three decomposed metrics: first word (3.95 s), tool call (2.21 s), and task completion (4.25 s). GPT-Realtime maintained moderate latency (6.89 s) while achieving high accuracy. Grok was competitive with a task completion latency of 6.65 s. Ultravox displayed an inverted latency profile, with a fast first-word latency (3.88 s) but a significantly slower tool-call latency (6.01 s) and task completion latency (8.40 s). This is largely attributable to its high filler rate (88.0%), where it speaks a filler sentence before initiating the API call. The Cascaded system was the slowest overall (10.12 s), primarily due to its first-word delay (8.78 s), confirming the bottleneck introduced by its sequential processing pipeline (Whisper→LLM→TTS).

**5.6. Qualitative Case Studies**
Two hard-difficulty scenarios illustrated the observed trade-offs. In a multi-step chain without disfluency (finance_18), Gemini Live 3.1 completed the task 2.3 times faster than GPT-Realtime, with both achieving perfect accuracy. Ultravox and the Cascaded pipeline showed ASR or reasoning errors. In a double self-correction scenario (travel_19), only GPT-Realtime correctly applied both mid-utterance corrections. Gemini Live 3.1's pre-emptive tool call led to it locking in the stale destination, while the Cascaded system's Whisper finalized the initial incorrect transcription. Ultravox interrupted the user with an unrelated filler while resolving the correction. These cases highlight the tension between eager processing for speed and conservative commitment for correctness when handling dynamic user intent.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The introduction of Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 (FDB-v3) represents a notable contribution to the evaluation of real-time voice agents. This benchmark is the first to systematically combine authentic human speech, multi-step tool execution, and detailed disfluency annotations in a reproducible framework. Its significance and potential impact can be delineated as follows:

**6.1. Addressing Critical Evaluation Gaps:**
FDB-v3 fills a significant void by moving beyond synthetic audio and single-step tasks. By incorporating real human speech with systematically annotated disfluencies (fillers, pauses, hesitations, false starts, self-corrections), it provides a more realistic and challenging environment for assessing voice agent robustness. The inclusion of multi-step function chaining across diverse task domains ensures that agents are evaluated on their ability to orchestrate complex, real-world actions, rather than just simple queries.

**6.2. Quantifying Performance Trade-offs:**
The detailed evaluation of six leading model configurations – including proprietary end-to-end systems and a traditional cascaded pipeline – provides empirical evidence for the inherent trade-offs between response speed, accuracy, and conversational flow. The results quantify how architectural choices impact these dimensions, demonstrating that while end-to-end models generally achieve lower latency, they introduce new challenges related to turn-taking and handling complex human speech.

**6.3. Highlighting Persistent Challenges in Self-Correction:**
A key finding is the consistent difficulty all models, including the leading proprietary systems, exhibit in handling mid-sentence self-corrections and dynamic state rollback. Even GPT-Realtime, the top performer, failed on over 40% of such scenarios. This identifies a fundamental limitation: the tendency of agents to commit to intermediate parameters prematurely, before a user's correction arrives. This insight directs future research toward developing more sophisticated state management and intent recognition mechanisms in real-time.

**6.4. Revealing Architectural and Behavioral Implications:**
The benchmark reveals distinct behavioral patterns and architectural implications. For instance, Gemini Live 3.1's "silent worker" phenomenon—where it executes tools but generates no speech in challenging scenarios—highlights a disconnect between reasoning and speech generation components. Ultravox's high filler rate combined with high interruption rate demonstrates a strategy to reduce perceived latency that often comes at the cost of conversational quality. Conversely, Grok's silent pre-processing shows a different approach to latency management. The Cascaded baseline's consistent turn-take but high latency starkly quantifies the overhead of traditional modular architectures compared to concurrent processing in end-to-end models.

**6.5. Guiding Future Research and Development:**
The findings suggest that the next frontier for voice agents is not solely latency reduction but a more nuanced balance between speed and flexibility. Future architectures must integrate fast tool execution with robust mechanisms to adapt to the unpredictable and dynamic nature of human conversation, particularly in handling evolving user intent. This includes improving models' abilities to distinguish provisional commitments from confirmed ones and implementing reliable state rollback capabilities.

**6.6. Promoting Reproducibility and Fair Comparison:**
By providing an open and reproducible benchmark, FDB-v3 enables researchers and developers to fairly compare their models against established baselines and leading systems. This fosters innovation by setting clear performance targets and allowing for systematic progress tracking in the field of real-time voice agents with tool-use capabilities.

In summary, FDB-v3 contributes a valuable tool for rigorous evaluation, deepens the understanding of current voice agent capabilities and limitations, and provides clear directions for the development of more intelligent, robust, and user-friendly spoken language interfaces capable of complex real-world interactions.
overview:
## The Evolution of Spoken AI Agents

The transition of Artificial Intelligence from text-based interfaces to interactive voice assistants represents a shift toward more natural human-computer interaction. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have become proficient at using external tools—such as checking a bank balance or booking a flight—through Application Programming Interfaces (APIs), bringing these capabilities to real-time voice agents introduces significant technical hurdles. Most existing voice systems operate in a "half-duplex" mode, where the user and the machine take turns speaking in a rigid sequence. In contrast, "full-duplex" agents can listen and speak simultaneously, allowing for interruptions and a more fluid conversational flow.

![Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04847v1/FDBv3.drawio.png)
*Figure 1: The Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 (FDB-v3) framework evaluates voice agents on their ability to execute multi-step tool calls while handling natural human speech phenomena such as fillers and self-corrections.*

This research introduces Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 (FDB-v3), a benchmark designed to evaluate how modern voice agents handle tool use under real-world conditions. Unlike previous benchmarks that often relied on synthetic, computer-generated voices, FDB-v3 utilizes authentic human audio recordings. These recordings include "disfluencies"—the natural stammers, pauses, and corrections that characterize human speech—to test the robustness of an agent's reasoning and its ability to execute complex tasks in real-time.

## Bridging the Gap: From Textual Logic to Verbal Interaction

In text-based environments, AI agents have ample time to process a prompt, reason through steps, and call tools. The input is usually clean and finalized. However, when an agent moves into the realm of spoken dialogue, it faces two primary challenges: latency and acoustic ambiguity. To feel natural, a voice agent must respond within milliseconds, yet it must also be accurate enough to avoid making permanent mistakes, such as booking a non-refundable ticket based on an incomplete sentence.

Current approaches to building voice agents typically fall into two categories. The "cascaded" approach pipes several models together: a Speech-to-Text (STT) model transcribes the audio, an LLM processes the text and decides on an action, and a Text-to-Speech (TTS) model generates the reply. While reliable, this chain introduces significant delays. The emerging "end-to-end" or speech-to-speech (S2S) models process audio signals directly into spoken responses. While these models are faster and can capture emotional nuances, they are often less transparent and can be more susceptible to errors caused by the messy nature of human speech. FDB-v3 provides a structured way to compare these different architectures.

## The Complexity of Human Speech: Disfluencies

One of the defining features of FDB-v3 is its focus on disfluencies. In a controlled laboratory setting, a user might say, "Please book me a flight to New York." In reality, a user might say, "Um, can you book me a flight to... let's see... New York? No, wait, I mean Boston." 

FDB-v3 categorizes these phenomena into five specific types to pinpoint where AI models fail:
*   **False Starts**: When a user begins a thought, abandons it, and starts a completely different request. The agent must discard the initial context to avoid executing incorrect tools.
*   **Self-Corrections**: When a user changes a specific detail (like a destination or a price range) mid-sentence. This requires "state rollback," where the agent must update its internal plan before calling an API.
*   **Fillers**: Words like "um" or "uh" that convey no information but maintain the floor. These can confuse some models, causing them to hallucinate tool parameters or delay their response.
*   **Pauses**: Silent intervals within a turn. A model that is too "eager" might interpret a pause as the end of the turn and interrupt the user.
*   **Hesitations**: Combinations of fillers and repetitions that test whether the agent can maintain focus on the core request.

By annotating 100 scenarios with these disfluencies across a variety of speakers, the benchmark creates a rigorous testbed for "dynamic state updates," ensuring that agents are not just following instructions but are actively listening and adapting to the user's evolving intent.

## Designing Realistic Agentic Tasks

To evaluate an agent’s practical utility, FDB-v3 employs a set of mock APIs across four distinct domains: Travel & Identity, Finance & Billing, Housing & Location, and E-Commerce Support. These APIs are deterministic—meaning they provide consistent, predictable outputs—which allows for objective scoring of the agent's decisions.

The benchmark organizes tasks into three levels of difficulty:
1.  **Easy**: Single-step tool calls with clear instructions.
2.  **Medium**: Two-step tasks requiring the agent to use the output of one tool as the input for another.
3.  **Hard**: Complex, multi-step chains with conflicting constraints or multiple required entity lookups.

For example, a "Hard" task in the Finance domain might involve checking a user's account balance, verifying their identification, and then authorizing a multi-part payment, all while the user is providing the information disfluently. This setup ensures that the benchmark tests not only the speech processing but also the underlying logical reasoning of the agent.

## Multi-Dimensional Evaluation Framework

Evaluating a voice agent requires more than just checking if it got the right answer. FDB-v3 uses a comprehensive set of metrics to capture the nuances of the interaction.

**Tool-Use Performance**
The core of the evaluation is based on whether the agent invoked the correct tools with the correct parameters. This is measured via:
*   **Tool Selection F1**: A balance between correctly identifying the needed tools and avoiding unnecessary ones.
*   **Argument Accuracy**: A measure of how well the agent extracted specific details (like dates or account numbers) from the audio.
*   **Task Completion ($\text{Pass@1}$)**: A strict binary metric. A scenario is only marked successful if every tool call and every argument is perfect.

**Turn-Taking and Latency**
In a full-duplex conversation, timing is everything. The benchmark measures:
*   **Turn-take rate**: How often the agent successfully provides a relevant response when the user stops speaking.
*   **Interruption rate**: How often the agent speaks over the user before they have finished their thought.
*   **Latency Decomposition**: To understand why an agent is slow, FDB-v3 breaks down the time spent into three parts:
    *   **First Word Latency**: The time until the agent makes any sound.
    *   **Tool Call Latency**: The time until the agent initiates the API call.
    *   **Task Completion Latency**: The total time until the agent delivers the final, factual result to the user.

If we denote the start time of the agent's response as $t_{\text{agent\_start}}$ and the end of the user's utterance as $t_{\text{user\_end}}$, the base latency is calculated as:
$$\Delta t = t_{\text{agent\_start}} - t_{\text{user\_end}}$$
If $\Delta t$ is negative, it indicates an interruption.

## Comparative Performance Analysis

The researchers tested several prominent models, including OpenAI's GPT-Realtime, Google's Gemini Live (versions 2.5 and 3.1), and open-weight models like Ultravox. They also included a "Cascaded" baseline using Whisper for transcription and GPT-4o for reasoning.

The results highlighted a clear trade-off between speed and correctness. GPT-Realtime emerged as the most accurate model, leading in tool selection and task completion ($\text{Pass@1}$ of $0.600$). However, Gemini Live 3.1 was significantly faster, achieving the lowest task completion latency ($4.25$ seconds compared to GPT-Realtime's $6.89$ seconds). 

A critical finding was the shared difficulty across all models in handling self-corrections. Even the most advanced models frequently "locked in" an incorrect parameter if the user corrected themselves late in the sentence. For instance, in a scenario where a user changed a travel destination, many agents had already initiated the search for the first destination before the user finished the correction. This "eagerness" is a byproduct of trying to minimize latency, but it leads to errors in dynamic environments.

The "Cascaded" system proved to be the most reliable at taking turns (a $100\%$ turn-take rate) but was by far the slowest, with a latency of over $10$ seconds. This confirms that while modular pipelines are robust, they struggle to meet the real-time demands of fluid conversation.

## Implications for Future Voice AI

The FDB-v3 benchmark reveals that the current frontier of voice AI is not just about making models faster, but about making them more flexible listeners. The "silent worker" phenomenon—where a model like Gemini Live 3.1 executes tools correctly but fails to speak to the user in complex situations—points to a disconnect between an agent's reasoning and its communicative layers.

Furthermore, the high interruption and filler rates seen in models like Ultravox suggest that using "filler speech" (saying things like "Let me check that for you...") is a common strategy to mask processing time. However, if these fillers are deployed incorrectly, they can disrupt the user and degrade the overall experience.

In conclusion, FDB-v3 provides a rigorous framework for identifying these specific failure modes. By moving the evaluation of voice agents into the messy, disfluent reality of human speech, the benchmark sets a standard for the next generation of assistants that must balance the need for speed with the necessity of being a careful, adaptive listener. Success in this domain will require agents that can not only understand what we say but also recognize when we change our minds.

keyInsights: Real-time voice agents face significant challenges in dynamically updating internal states and correctly processing mid-utterance self-corrections, often committing to parameters prematurely before user corrections are fully registered.
There are inherent trade-offs between processing speed, overall accuracy in tool execution, and maintaining natural conversational flow and turn-taking dynamics in full-duplex interactions.

Different architectural approaches and processing strategies (e.g., eager execution, silent pre-processing, high filler usage) result in distinct behavioral patterns and performance characteristics, each with unique implications for user experience.
originalProblem: Most existing spoken dialogue systems primarily focus on conversational chat and lack the capacity to invoke external APIs or execute practical actions on behalf of the user.

Current benchmarks for voice agents often rely on synthetic audio, omit natural speech disfluencies, or are restricted to single-step tasks, failing to represent complex, real-world human-agent interactions.
Proprietary real-time voice agents, despite their capabilities, lack rigorous evaluation under controlled, reproducible conditions, hindering transparent performance comparisons.
results: GPT-Realtime exhibited the strongest overall performance with a Task Completion (Pass@1) of 0.600, leading in tool selection F1 (0.876) and argument accuracy (0.680), and maintaining the lowest interruption rate (13.5%).

Gemini Live 3.1 achieved the fastest task completion latency (4.25 s) but had the lowest turn-take rate (78.0%), while the traditional Cascaded baseline incurred the highest latency (10.12 s) due to sequential processing.

Performance consistently degraded for all models as scenario difficulty increased and with the presence of disfluencies, particularly self-corrections, where even the top-performing GPT-Realtime scored 0.588.
solution: Developed Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 (FDB-v3), a benchmark comprising 100 multi-step tool-use scenarios across four diverse task domains, including 21 scenarios specifically designed to test self-correction capabilities.

Collected and systematically annotated real human audio data, incorporating natural disfluencies like false starts, self-corrections, fillers, pauses, and hesitations, to assess model robustness in realistic acoustic conditions.

Employed locally executed mock APIs with deterministic, zero-latency responses to isolate model reasoning and parameter-passing from external factors, and a multi-dimensional evaluation framework to assess accuracy, latency, and conversational dynamics.
summary.summary: Full-Duplex-Bench-v3 (FDB-v3) introduces a benchmark for real-time voice agents performing multi-step tool execution using authentic human speech with systematic disfluency annotations. Its evaluation of six leading models quantifies the trade-offs between processing speed, accuracy, and conversational dynamics, revealing persistent challenges in handling dynamic user intent and self-corrections.

title: Full-Duplex-Bench-v3: Benchmarking Tool Use for Full-Duplex Voice Agents Under Real-World Disfluency
